title: Whisky for rainy April days: our favourites for gloomy moments
html_content:
Whisky for rainy April days: our favourites for both grey and sunny moments

April is unpredictable. No sooner do you think spring has finally arrived than a grey cloud front rolls in and the rain starts drumming against the window pane again. For us – Steffen and Ellas – that’s no cause for complaint. On the contrary: a rainy April day is the perfect excuse to open a bottle of whisky you’ve been saving for a while. A warm light, a good book or simply the silence – and a glass that tastes of more than just alcohol.

We’ve spent a lot of time in Scotland – and as we all know, it rains a lot in Scotland. On our travels from Islay to the Orkney Islands, we’ve learnt that the right whisky for the right weather turns a grey day into something special. In this article, we’ll introduce you to our personal favourites for rainy April days – complete with tasting notes, honest reviews and, of course, buying recommendations.

What makes a whisky the perfect companion for a rainy day?

Not every whisky suits every moment. Light, floral summer drams feel out of place in the pouring rain. What scores highly on a rainy April day are whiskies with depth, warmth and body. So we’re looking for:

  • Warming body: Whiskies at 46% ABV or higher that unfold on the palate and don’t evaporate straight away.
  • Spice and depth: Sherry notes, dark fruits, dried fruits, spices such as cinnamon, cloves and ginger.
  • Optional smokiness: For us, light to medium peat is welcome on rainy days – it evokes Scottish evenings by the fireside and wet moorland.
  • Long finish: The finish should linger.

1. GlenDronach 18 Year Old Allardice – the velvety sherry dream

If there is one whisky that feels like a heavy woollen jumper on a rainy day, it is the GlenDronach 18 Year Old Allardice. It is matured exclusively in Oloroso sherry casks – no exceptions, no compromise. The result is a depth that is second to none: dried plums, dark chocolate, raisins, a hint of leather and, right at the very end, a delicate note of coffee.

Steffen opened this bottle for the first time during a visit to the GlenDronach distillery in Aberdeenshire – a day when it was literally pouring with rain. Perfect conditions. Ellas has had a bottle of it at home ever since. 46% ABV, natural colour, non-chill filtered.

Order GlenDronach 18 Year Old Allardice now

2. Benromach 10 Years – Speyside with a hint of peat

Benromach is one of Speyside’s smallest distilleries and, at the same time, one of the most idiosyncratic. The 10-year-old matures in ex-bourbon and sherry casks, lending it notes of vanilla, nougat, pear compote and a hint of spice. 43% ABV – a wonderful everyday whisky for rainy days, which requires little effort yet still gives you food for thought. Like us, this distillery also had a royal visitor at its opening. On a tour of the distillery, this story is sure to be recounted to you with great pride.

Benromach 10 Years Old – the underrated Speyside

3. Talisker 18 Years Old – Storm in a glass, fire in the belly

For us, Talisker is closely associated with the Isle of Skye – and with rain. It rains a lot on Skye, the wind lashes across the mountains, and the landscape possesses a raw, wild beauty that takes your breath away. The Talisker 18 Years Old captures exactly that: it begins with a salty sea breeze, develops into smoky peaty notes and ends with a long, peppery-warm explosion on the palate – and is one of Steffen’s favourite whiskies

Talisker 18 Years Old – Skye in a glass

4. Glenfarclas 15 Years Old – the cosy Scottish living room

For us, Glenfarclas is a home, not a whisky. The Speyside distillery has been run by the Grant family for generations, and you can sense that in every sip: this isn’t about trends or elaborate marketing campaigns, but about continuity and quality. The 15-year-old is matured exclusively in first-fill sherry casks. The result: Christmas cake, marzipan, ripe figs, a hint of orange peel and a pleasant spiciness. 46% ABV, full-bodied and warming.

Ellas calls it the Sunday whisky – and that really says it all.

Glenfarclas 15 Year Old – Speyside’s Sherrywood classic

5. Bowmore 12 Year Old – the gentle side of Islay

Not everyone likes the intense peat of an Ardbeg or Laphroaig – but to miss out on Islay altogether would be a mistake. Bowmore is the compromise: enough peat to convey that maritime Islay feel, yet gentle enough to appeal even to newcomers to the smoky world. The 12 Year Old impresses with an unusual combination: salty peaty notes meet floral tones and a creamy chocolate base. 40% ABV.

Bowmore 12 Years Old – Islay for every occasion

6. Springbank 10 Years – Campbeltown and the sea

Springbank from Campbeltown is one of those whiskies that even connoisseurs pause to appreciate. The distillery does almost everything in-house: malting, distilling, maturing, bottling – and all in small batches with a great deal of craftsmanship. The 10-year-old is their entry-level expression and, at the same time, one of the most characterful whiskies in this price range. Salty, slightly peaty, with oils and brininess. 46% ABV, naturally uncoloured and non-chill-filtered.

Springbank 10 Year Old – Craftsmanship from Campbeltown

7. Dalmore 12 Year Old – Highland luxury for the home

The Dalmore is renowned for its distinctive bottle featuring a golden stag’s head. The 12 Year Old is matured in a combination of American white oak casks and sherry finish casks, giving it a creamy, chocolatey depth. Notes of dark chocolate, orange zest, cinnamon and a hint of honeyed sweetness make the Dalmore 12 Year Old a whisky that also appeals to those just beginning their whisky journey. 40% ABV.

Dalmore 12 Year Old – Highland elegance with a sherry finish

Rain isn’t bad weather – rain is the reason why Scotland is so beautifully green. And why we love drinking whisky so much. – Steffen and Ella

The perfect ritual: how to make the most of a rainy day

  • The right glass: A nosing glass (tulip shape) concentrates the aromas. We recommend the classic Glencairn glass.
  • No ice, please: Ice masks the aromas. If you like, add a small drop of still water.
  • Taste slowly: First smell (nose), then hold the first sip on your tongue, then swallow and observe the finish.
  • Accompaniments: Dark chocolate (70%+), a piece of good cheese or simply silence.

Glencairn whisky nosing glasses – the original from Scotland

Our recommendation depending on your mood

  • Contemplative and calm: Glenfarclas 15 Years or GlenDronach 18 Years Allardice
  • Adventurous and travel-loving: Talisker 18 Years or Springbank 10 Years
  • Relaxed and cosy: Benromach 10 Years or Dalmore 12 Years
  • Looking for Islay without the intense peat: Bowmore 12 Years

Conclusion: April needs the right whisky

April has a gift: it forces us to slow down. Those who are always out and about in the bright sunshine finally find time on rainy days for home, for a book, for a chat – and for a really good whisky. We hope our list inspires you to welcome the next raincloud not with displeasure, but with a carefully chosen dram. Cheers – may the rain last a long time!

teaser: April is showing its wet side once again. No problem – we’ve picked out seven whiskies that taste particularly good on grey, rainy days. With tasting notes and buying recommendations.
meta_description: Steffen and Ella recommend seven whiskies for rainy April days: from GlenDronach to Talisker and Springbank – complete with tasting notes and tips for the perfect whisky ritual.
